title: Functions
beginnerExplanation: Reusable blocks of code with a name, which can take inputs (parameters) and give back outputs (return values).
intermediateExplanation: To avoid writing the same logic multiple times. If you have a complex attack calculation, you write it once as a function, then call it whenever needed.

realWorldAnalogy.title: A Vending Machine
realWorldAnalogy.description: You give it input (money, button press), it does some internal work, and it returns an output (a snack). You don't need to know how the machine works inside to use it.

mentalModel: You give it input (money, button press), it does some internal work, and it returns an output (a snack). You don't need to know how the machine works inside to use it.
visualDiagram:
graph TD
  Start --> Process[Functions]
  Process --> End
executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the "return" statement, which causes the function to return None.
Using variables defined inside a function outside of it (Scope error).
Forgetting parentheses when calling the function.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Functions but hit a common pitfall: Forgetting the "return" statement, which causes the function to return None.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Functions should do exactly one thing well.

practiceQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the "return" keyword?
options: To print output to the console.
To send a value back to where the function was called.
To go back to the top of the loop.
practiceQuestions.explanation: The return keyword ends function execution and passes the result back to the caller.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Functions?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

projectExercise.title: Mastering Functions
projectExercise.description: Implement a clean snippet demonstrating the use of Functions.
requirements:
Write a snippet utilizing the syntax: def my_function(param1):
    return result

bossBattle.name: The Functions Guardian
bossBattle.description: A guardian protecting the secrets of Functions. Use your knowledge to bypass its security gates.
bossBattle.mechanic: Resolve syntax bugs and refactor code structure.

artifacts.name: Scroll of Functions
artifacts.description: A scroll detailing the core principles of Functions.

masteryChecklist: I understand how to implement Functions.
I can avoid Forgetting the "return" statement, which causes the function to return None..
confusionPoints: Forgetting the "return" statement, which causes the function to return None.
Using variables defined inside a function outside of it (Scope error).
Forgetting parentheses when calling the function.
simplifiedExplanation: Reusable blocks of code with a name, which can take inputs (parameters) and give back outputs (return values).

retentionSystem.revision1: Define the core syntax of Functions.
retentionSystem.revision2: Name one common mistake when using Functions.

developerMode.industryUsage: Functions is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Functions should do exactly one thing well.
Return values instead of printing them, so other code can use the result.
antiPatterns: Forgetting the "return" statement, which causes the function to return None.
Using variables defined inside a function outside of it (Scope error).
Forgetting parentheses when calling the function.
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
